mappings.rationaleAI: Genghis Khan's unification of Mongol tribes, military innovations, conquests across China, Central Asia, Persia, and Eastern Europe, the Yasa legal code, and the Pax Mongolica directly correspond to C.1.a (rise of Genghis Khan) and C.1.b (division of his empire).

The Great Wall's defence against nomadic peoples (Xiongnu, Mongols) and its relationship to the Silk Road are relevant to B.1 (Hsiung-nu tribal confederation and Chinese pressure) and E (waning of nomad power and Manchu conquest).

Marco Polo's journey along the Silk Road to Kublai Khan's Yuan dynasty court, his claimed service to the Mongol emperor, and the book's influence on European knowledge of Asia directly correspond to C.2.a (completion of conquest of China and the Yuan dynasty under Kublai Khan) and B.2.b (Yuan China and the West).

The Battle of Talas (751) between the Abbasid Caliphate and Tang Dynasty in Central Asia, establishing Islamic influence in the region, is relevant to B.3.a on West Turkistan and the advent of Muslim rule.

The Prester John legend's location in Asia and its influence on European exploration are relevant to the broader context of C.1.b (Mangu Khan's friendly relations with Western Christendom) and European understanding of Inner Asian powers.
